kpi_cards.label: LME CASH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: Latest published OFFICIAL remains Fri 10-Jul (Westmetall): $3,156.00 vs 09-Jul $3,164.00 = −$8.00 (−0.25%). Monday's official prints 13:20 London, AFTER this run — nothing in this number reflects the weekend. Since Friday: the IRGC declared Hormuz CLOSED, the US struck ~140 targets Saturday plus a further wave Sunday, and Iran hit US bases in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman. ~18.1% below the 02-Jun four-year high ($3,855).

kpi_cards.label: LME 3-MONTH ($/t)
kpi_cards.delta: 10-Jul official $3,153.00 (−$16.00, −0.50%) — the last print before the blockade. The cash–3M spread stands at +$3.00 BACKWARDATION (from −$5 contango), the first positive front spread since June's war-premium regime. Resistance stack $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline); $3,050 is the structural line below.

kpi_cards.label: LME STOCK (t)
kpi_cards.delta: 10-Jul official 287,725 t — a further −1,500 t draw and the 13th straight week of net withdrawals (509kt on 02-Jan → 288kt, −43.5% YTD). A shrinking visible buffer is precisely what turns a transit disruption into a price event: the exchange has less cover than at any point in this cycle.

kpi_cards.label: BRENT ($/bbl)
kpi_cards.delta: Brent traded ~3.5–4% higher at about $78.7–79 on Monday (from Friday's $76.01 settle), snapping a two-day losing streak after the weekend strikes and the IRGC closure declaration (CNBC / Bloomberg, 12–13-Jul). Energy is the largest smelter cost line and the cleanest real-time read on the war premium the metal has not yet priced.

benchmark.name: LME Cash settlement
benchmark.note: Fri 10-Jul OFFICIAL (Westmetall) — still the latest PUBLISHED official at run time (Mon 06:05 London; Monday's official prints 13:20). $3,156.00 vs 09-Jul $3,164.00 = −$8.00 (−0.25%). This print PRE-DATES the weekend: Hormuz declared closed, two US strike waves, Iranian missile/drone attacks on US bases in four Gulf states. avg = June-2026 monthly mean ($3,458.6).

benchmark.name: LME 3-Month
benchmark.note: 10-Jul official $3,153.00 vs 09-Jul $3,169.00 (−$16.00, −0.50%); Friday's later closing print was $3,139.5. The .50 retrace of the $3,065→$3,210.5 bounce ($3,138) held on a closing basis. $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline) upgrades the count; $3,050 re-opens the downside. avg = June-2026 3M mean ($3,438.9).

benchmark.name: Cash–3M spread
benchmark.note: BACKWARDATION (+$3.00): cash $3,156.00 over 3M $3,153.00. Convention: cash above 3M = backwardation = nearby tightness. It flipped positive on Friday — BEFORE the strait was declared closed — with stock at 287,725 t. Whether Monday's official WIDENS this spread is the single most informative print of the week: a widening backwardation confirms the physical market is repricing the blockade, a snap back to contango says the market is treating it as noise.

premiums.name: MJP Q3 (Main Japanese Port)
premiums.src: Q3-2026 SETTLED at $395/t (S&P Global Platts, assessed 06-Jul) — the highest quarterly Japan premium in a decade (+12% q/q vs Q2's $350–353). Spot has since printed BELOW settlement ($355, SMM 10-Jul). The weekend's formal Hormuz closure is the one development capable of arresting that slide before Q4 talks open (~Sep). Source: Platts via IndexBox. avg = Q1-26 settlement ($195).

premiums.name: CIF Japan spot (MJP spot)
premiums.src: Latest public SMM weekly print (10-Jul): Japan MJP ingot spot premium $355/t, down from $380 — buyers restocking for immediate needs only. Assessed BEFORE the IRGC closure declaration; the next weekly print (Fri 17-Jul) is the first to carry the blockade. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0343 / SMM.

premiums.name: European Duty Unpaid (EDU)
premiums.src: Platts daily P1020A duty-UNPAID in-whs Rotterdam last publicly assessed $525–560/t (mid $542.50, 03-Jul) — no fresher public print at run time. Structural floor holds: 6% EU duty + CBAM + Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance. Gulf war-risk freight, the marginal swing factor, is re-widening after the weekend (S&P Global / SMM).

premiums.name: Billets Rotterdam Duty-Paid
premiums.src: Value-added 6063 billet conversion premium, in-whs duty-paid Rotterdam — LAST PUBLIC REFERENCE (late-Jun; no fresh public print at run time). Conversion premia are stickier than ingot premia and Mozal remains on care & maintenance. Verify Fastmarkets MB-AL-0002.

premiums.name: MW US Transaction (Platts)
premiums.src: Platts US MW Transaction premium last publicly assessed 108.7¢/lb ≈ $2,396/t (03-Jul; prior public print 119.05¢, 20-May). The tariff-$ leg moves MECHANICALLY with the LME (≈$1,578/t at the 10-Jul cash of $3,156 × 50%), so any Hormuz-driven LME gap lifts the US all-in regardless of physical demand. SMM's 10-Jul weekly shows DDP drifting 110.25→109.75¢. Verify S&P Global.

premiums.name: US Midwest Duty-Paid (P1020A)
premiums.src: SMM weekly DDP print 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul, from 110.25¢). Duty-paid = LME + Section-232 tariff (50%) + market premium. Policy, not physical tightness, is ~two-thirds of this number; the residual market premium is easing with the US off-season. Verify Platts/Fastmarkets.

macro.name: Brent crude ($/bbl)
macro.note: Brent traded ~3.5–4% higher at about $78.67–79 on Monday (from Friday's $76.01 settle), snapping a two-day losing streak after the weekend exchange of strikes and the IRGC's closure declaration. Energy is the largest single smelter cost line and the cleanest real-time read on the war premium the metal has not yet priced (CNBC / Bloomberg, 12–13-Jul).

macro.name: US Dollar Index (DXY)
macro.note: Closed the prior week around 100.5–101. A weekend risk-off gap is USD-supportive at the margin, which cuts against dollar-priced metals even as the supply story turns bullish — the two forces partly offset. US CPI (Tue) is the week's macro pivot; the Fed framework review continues to de-emphasise forward guidance.

macro.name: EUR/USD
macro.note: Near 1.1420. The ECB raised its key rate to 2.25% in June — the first major central bank to tighten explicitly on war-driven energy costs. A renewed Gulf flare and a ~$79 Brent keep that energy-inflation channel live and argue against a near-term reversal of that stance.

macro.name: USD/CNY
macro.note: Yuan steady ~6.77. China's June trade data — including unwrought aluminium exports — is due TODAY (13-Jul) and is the week's first hard demand datapoint. The import window remains shut; June CPI +1.0% / PPI +4.1% keeps easing on the table (SMM / NBS / customs calendar).

macro.name: USD/JPY
macro.note: Yen around 161.70. A weak yen marginally supports Japan-bound cargo premia in local terms — though MJP spot still fell to $355. The larger Q4-talks variable is now whether Gulf metal can physically reach Asian buyers at all.

macro.name: US 10Y yield (%)
macro.note: Closed the prior week at 4.56%. CME FedWatch (via SMM, 10-Jul): July hold ~74.9%; September cumulative-hike odds ~64%. A sustained oil gap re-tightens the rates ceiling — an energy-driven inflation impulse is the mechanism by which a bullish metal story can turn into a macro headwind for metals.

macro_events: HORMUZ DECLARED CLOSED (Sun 12-Jul): the IRGC Navy said the Strait of Hormuz is closed 'until further notice' after firing on a Cyprus-flagged container ship; CENTCOM called it a blatant attack, disputed the closure, and launched further strikes to enforce freedom of navigation. Pre-war, the Gulf supplied ~9% of global and ~25% of ex-China primary aluminium, ~80–85% of it routed through this strait (Al-Monitor / CNN / Al Jazeera).

STRIKES AND COUNTER-STRIKES: US forces hit ~140 Iranian military targets on Saturday and launched a further wave on Sunday. Iran responded Sunday with strikes on US military facilities in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman (Tasnim, via CNBC) — the first time this round of the conflict has put ordnance over four Gulf host states at once, three of which host aluminium capacity.

OIL REPRICES, METAL HAS NOT (YET): Brent gapped ~3.5–4% to about $79 on Monday. Aluminium's last published LME official is Friday's ($3,156 cash / $3,153 3M) — Monday's official prints at 13:20 London, after this run. The gap between what oil has priced and what the metal has priced is the tradable observation of the day (CNBC / Bloomberg).

THE WEEK'S HARD DATES: China June trade data (incl. unwrought aluminium exports) TODAY 13-Jul; US CPI Tue 14-Jul; Alcoa Q2 Thu 16-Jul (after NYSE close); US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline Fri 17-Jul; the next overseas premium weeklies Fri 17-Jul — the first assessments to carry the blockade.

THE DE-ESCALATION TRIGGER: Washington has demanded a public Iranian statement declaring the strait open for all traffic, and a senior US official has conditioned the entire nuclear track on free tanker transit. Muscat remains the venue. Until that statement lands, treat every premium and freight assumption as provisional (Al Jazeera live blog).
lme_commentary: The metal is trading blind into the biggest supply-side headline of the quarter. Friday's officials — cash $3,156.00 (−0.25%), 3M $3,153.00 (−0.50%) — remain the latest published prints at run time (Monday's official lands 13:20 London), and they PRE-DATE everything that matters: the IRGC declaring the Strait of Hormuz closed 'until further notice', two waves of US strikes (~140 targets Saturday, more Sunday), and Iranian missile and drone attacks on US bases in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman. Oil has already answered — Brent gapped ~4% to about $79 — while aluminium's last mark still sits where a de-escalating market left it. Underneath the flat price, the plumbing had already turned: the cash–3M spread flipped to +$3.00 BACKWARDATION on Friday (from −$5 contango) with stock drawn to 287,725 t, the 13th consecutive week of withdrawals and −43.5% year-to-date. That is a market with less visible cover than at any point in this cycle, now facing a formally contested transit route for ~25% of ex-China supply. The technical map: $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline) is the resistance stack whose closing break upgrades the count toward $3,400; $3,050 is the structural line below. The honest caveat is the physical tape — every overseas premium print last week was lower and China is destocking into its off-season, so escalation has to overcome an adequately supplied market rather than a starved one. Watch whether Monday's official WIDENS the backwardation: that, not the flat price, is the tell.

alumina.name: Alumina FOB Australia (SMM/Fastmarkets)
alumina.src: ~$340/t FOB East Australia — latest public reference. Oversupply DEEPENS: EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul (~50% within days, full capability targeted end-2026); Nalco guides $320–330/t for 2026. The bearish commodity story is intact — but the delivered-cost leg (Gulf freight, war-risk) just got worse. Verify SMM/Fastmarkets.

alumina.name: Alumina API / Platts region
alumina.src: Ex-China benchmark region ~$304/t — easing on the building surplus (latest public reference). EGA's refinery return adds ~2.4 Mtpa back to ex-China supply through H2. Verify Platts.

alumina.name: SHFE alumina futures (AO2609)
alumina.src: Last public print in the ¥2,700s (SMM); price below all moving averages with the bearish alignment intact and port stocks building on high ex-China arrivals. Monday reopens with two opposing forces: the EGA-restart surplus versus a formally closed Hormuz — note ~60% of Gulf alumina imports transit the strait, so a blockade squeezes GULF refinery feed even as the global balance loosens (SMM).

alumina.name: Platts FOB Australia (futures)
alumina.src: Alumina FOB Australia (Platts) futures ~$309/t — CME/TradingView; no material move in the latest public print.

alumina_notes: The alumina market is the mirror image of metal: LOOSENING while metal tightens. EGA's Al Taweelah refinery (2.4 Mtpa) restarted 10-Jul, Guinea bauxite is in oversupply (CIF China ~$68/dmt) and Chinese port stocks are building — ex-China alumina should consolidate in the $300–340 band.

The blockade cuts the other way for GULF refineries specifically: roughly 60% of regional alumina imports transit Hormuz. A sustained closure constrains feed into Gulf smelters even while the global alumina balance stays long — a regional squeeze inside a global surplus.

Guinea's pending export-quota policy (a push toward a >$100/dmt bauxite target) remains the one clear bullish catalyst on the cost curve; Metro Mining's record 1.8 Mt Q2 shipments underline how much length is currently in the seaborne market.
net_read: OIL HAS PRICED IT; METAL HASN'T. Brent is ~4% higher at about $79 on a formally declared Hormuz closure, two US strike waves and Iranian attacks on US bases across four Gulf states — while aluminium's last published official ($3,153 3M) still reflects a de-escalating Friday. Beneath it, the front spread had ALREADY flipped to +$3 backwardation on a 287,725 t stock base. Net: near-term risk is skewed to the upside and the $3,230/$3,260 stack is the test, but this is a headline-fragile move — an Iranian 'strait open' statement out of the Muscat track (Washington's explicit demand) reverses it as fast as it came, and last week's premium slide says the physical market outside the Gulf is not yet short.

feed.text: STRAIT DECLARED CLOSED: the IRGC Navy said the Strait of Hormuz is shut 'until further notice' after firing on a Cyprus-flagged container ship (one crew member missing). CENTCOM called it a blatant attack, disputed the closure and launched further strikes to ensure freedom of navigation (Al-Monitor / CNN / KVIA). Price-bullish: the transit artery for ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports is now formally contested.

IRAN STRIKES US BASES IN FOUR GULF STATES: following a second US strike wave (after ~140 targets hit Saturday), Iran launched strikes on US military facilities in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman (Tasnim, via CNBC). Bahrain and Oman host regional aluminium capacity — the conflict has moved from the waterway to the host countries.

OIL GAPS ~4%: Brent traded ~3.5–4% higher at about $78.67–79, snapping a two-day losing streak (CNBC / Bloomberg). Energy is the largest smelter cost line; a sustained move here feeds the cost curve AND the rates ceiling.

SHIPPING: the Combined Maritime Forces' Joint Maritime Information Center advisory holds the Hormuz security threat level at SEVERE, while EXPANDING the southern Omani-coast transit route to two-way traffic — a partial corridor, not a reopening. Expect war-risk surcharges to re-widen and quote validity to shorten before any assessed premium moves.

LAST PUBLISHED OFFICIALS (Westmetall): Al cash $3,156.00 (−0.25%) / 3M $3,153.00 (−0.50%); the cash–3M spread flipped to +$3.00 BACKWARDATION and stock drew to 287,725 t (−43.5% YTD). Base complex: Cu $13,454, Ni $16,600, Zn $3,588, Pb $1,890. Monday's official prints 13:20 London — after this run.

PHYSICAL TAPE STILL SOFT (pre-blockade): SMM's weekly shows every overseas premium lower — Japan MJP spot $355/t (from $380, and below the $395 settled Q3), US Midwest DDP 109.75¢/lb. EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah alumina refinery restarted 10-Jul. The bearish supply-repair evidence is real; it was simply all assessed before the strait closed.

news.headline: BLOCKADE DECLARED: the IRGC Navy says the Strait of Hormuz is closed 'until further notice' after firing on a Cyprus-flagged container ship; CENTCOM disputes the closure and struck ~140 Iranian targets Saturday plus a further wave Sunday. Iran retaliated against US bases in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman. Washington's stated de-escalation condition is a public Iranian declaration that the strait is open — and a senior US official has tied the entire nuclear track to free tanker transit. Next hard date: the US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down closes 17-Jul.
news.source: Al-Monitor (12-Jul); CNN live updates (11-Jul); Al Jazeera live blog; CNBC (12-Jul)

news.headline: BRENT GAPS ~4% TO ~$79: oil snapped a two-day losing streak on the weekend strikes, reversing the drift back toward pre-war levels. For aluminium this is a double transmission — energy is the largest smelter cost input, and a sustained oil-led inflation impulse tightens the rates ceiling that has been capping dollar-priced metals.

METAL HAS NOT REPRICED YET: the latest published LME official remains Friday's (cash $3,156 / 3M $3,153; stock 287,725 t) — Monday's prints at 13:20 London, after this run. The cash–3M spread had already flipped to +$3 BACKWARDATION on Friday. The tell to watch is not the flat price but whether Monday's official WIDENS that backwardation.

THE PHYSICAL TAPE DISAGREES (for now): every overseas premium print last week was lower — Japan MJP spot $355/t (below the $395 settled Q3), US Midwest DDP 109.75¢/lb, Europe easing — while China destocked at a four-year-high pace into its off-season and EGA's 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery restarted. All of it was assessed BEFORE the closure. The 17-Jul weeklies are the first prints to carry the blockade.
news.source: SMM weekly (10-Jul) — news.metal.com; S&P Global Platts

news.headline: THREAT LEVEL SEVERE, SOUTHERN CORRIDOR ONLY: the Combined Maritime Forces' JMIC advisory (12-Jul) holds Hormuz at SEVERE while expanding the southern Omani-coast route to two-way traffic. War-risk surcharges re-widen, exclusion clauses re-activate and quote validity shortens — the delivered-cost leg of every Gulf-origin cargo deteriorates before any assessed premium moves.
news.source: Combined Maritime Forces JMIC advisory (12-Jul), via Al Jazeera live blog

earnings.headline: Revenue $3.2bn (−7% q/q); net income $425m ($1.60/sh); adj. EBITDA $595m; cash $1.4bn.
earnings.readthrough: Higher Al prices + operational resilience amid Gulf disruption; San Ciprián restart completed Apr 2026. 01-Jul: agreed to acquire South32's bauxite/alumina/aluminium portfolio (Boddington, Worsley, MRN, Alumar, South Africa ops) for $4.1bn upfront ($3.1bn cash + ~$1.0bn stock; up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 — making Alcoa the world's largest bauxite miner. Q2 results CONFIRMED for Thu 16-Jul (after NYSE close; call 5pm EDT — BusinessWire).

earnings.headline: Adj. EBITDA NOK 8,668m (vs 9,516m yr-ago); adj. EPS NOK 2.07 (beat, up from 1.63).
earnings.readthrough: Primary Al EBITDA +2.7% YoY as Norwegian smelters reopened; soft alumina prices & stronger NOK; Extrusions outlook cut. Q2 due 22-Jul (07:00 CEST).

earnings.headline: Adj. EPS $1.63 (beat $1.56); revenue $649m; net income $327m (incl. $288m Hawesville sale gain).
earnings.readthrough: Higher LME + strong US Midwest premiums; Q2 adj. EBITDA guided $315–335m.

earnings.headline: Net profit $200.3m (+316% YoY); EPS 53 fils; sales −17% on Gulf supply-chain disruption.
earnings.readthrough: LME averaged $3,195/t (+22% YoY) drove profit surge; volumes hit by regional conflict; ~19% capacity in controlled shutdown — restart path improves post-truce.

earnings.headline: Net profit SAR 1.63bn (~$436m, +5.5% YoY); revenue SAR 8.79bn (+3.2%); EBITDA $964m.
earnings.readthrough: Strong pricing across commodities; partly offset by lower phosphate volumes; 2026 capex set >$4bn.

earnings.headline: Aluminium 835kt (+1% YoY); alumina +6%; bauxite −11% (Cyclone Narelle at Weipa/Gove); AP60 first hot metal.
earnings.readthrough: Flags Gulf curtailments = 23% of ex-China supply; expects enhanced 2026 deficit; Kitimat/AP60 ramp-up.

earnings.headline: Hillside Al ~538kt (9M, FY26 guide 720kt); Brazil Alumina record 1,060kt (9M); Worsley steady.
earnings.readthrough: Alumina sales −15% q/q on availability/timing; annual guidance intact despite cyclone gas disruption.

earnings.headline: Hindalco PAT ₹5,283cr (+66% YoY); India Al upstream EBITDA margin ~46%; Novelis FY26 adj. EBITDA $1.6bn (−9%).
earnings.readthrough: Record India operations; Novelis pressured by Oswego outage (~$104m) and tariffs (~$143m).

earnings.headline: Net profit guided RMB 5.30–5.59bn (+50–58% YoY).
earnings.readthrough: Strong on higher Al prices; China output near self-imposed 45 Mt capacity cap.

earnings.headline: Underlying profit $6.2bn (+>20%); underlying EBITDA $15.5bn (+25%, 58% margin); interim div 73c.
earnings.readthrough: Copper-led strength; broad base-metals read-across positive for the complex.

earnings.headline: FY25 results; copper & aluminium average prices +9% YoY; zinc metal +4%.
earnings.readthrough: Resilient structural demand for Cu/Al cited; broadly constructive sector tone.

earnings.headline: FY25 net loss $455m (vs $803m profit in 2024) on higher costs & lower output.
earnings.readthrough: Analysts expect stronger H1 2026 on higher prices; Asia pivot amid fractured trade flows.

earnings.headline: Guides H1-26 net profit ¥1.8–1.95bn, +154.4–175.6% YoY.
earnings.readthrough: Credits industry-chain cost reduction, aluminium prices fluctuating at highs, high-value-added deep-processing mix and energy-saving retrofits — the first sizeable China H1 pre-announcement of the season and a positive read-across for Chinese producer margins.

earnings_note: Earnings are quarterly/episodic; entries show each listed peer's most recent reported results as of 13-Jul (public, listed-company information). Q2 season opens this week: Alcoa Thu 16-Jul (after NYSE close), Rio Tinto Q2 production report mid-week (H1 results 13-Aug), Norsk Hydro 22-Jul (07:00 CEST), Maaden (1211) H1 late-Jul/early-Aug (Tadawul). Corporate action: the Alcoa–South32 bauxite/alumina/aluminium transaction — $4.1bn upfront (up to $5.6bn with contingent payments to 2030), close expected H1-2027 (announced 01-Jul). Context for the prints: Q2 average LME cash ≈ $3,459 (June) versus $3,156 now — sequentially strong realised prices with a fading tail. With Hormuz declared closed, the two disclosures that matter most this season are what producers say about Gulf-region insurance and freight costs, and about US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% Section-232 tariff.

commercial.text: The observable gap this morning is that oil has repriced the blockade (~+4%, Brent ~$79) and aluminium has not — its last published official is Friday's, struck before the closure was declared. Monday's 13:20 London official is the first metal print to carry the news. The resistance stack is $3,230 (MA20) → $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline); a CLOSING break above $3,260 is what would upgrade a headline bounce into a trend. $3,050 remains the structural line below. Technical context, not advice.

Every overseas premium print in the market's current data set (MJP spot $355 vs the $395 settled Q3; US MW DDP 109.75¢; Europe easing) was assessed BEFORE the strait was declared closed. Those marks should not be extrapolated forward this week. The 17-Jul weeklies are the first assessments to carry the blockade, and Q4 MJP talks (~Sep) now open against both a decade-high settled anchor and an actively contested transit route. In the US, the tariff leg (50% × LME) means the all-in rises mechanically with any LME gap, independent of physical demand.

The alumina commodity story stays bearish (EGA's 2.4 Mtpa restart; FOB Australia ~$340, API ~$304; SHFE AO below all MAs) — but the regional wrinkle is that ~60% of Gulf alumina imports transit Hormuz, so a sustained closure squeezes GULF refinery and smelter feed inside a globally long market. Watch the divergence between the index and the delivered cost.

The Combined Maritime Forces hold the Hormuz threat at SEVERE with only an expanded southern Omani corridor operating. War-risk surcharges, exclusion clauses and quote validity are the fastest-moving cost lines in the chain right now — faster than any assessed premium. Anyone modelling Gulf-origin delivered costs off last week's numbers is modelling a market that no longer exists.

bottom_line: On 13-Jul the market's two clocks are out of sync. Oil has already repriced a declared Hormuz closure — Brent ~4% higher at about $79 after two US strike waves and Iranian attacks on US bases in four Gulf states — while aluminium's last published official ($3,156 cash / $3,153 3M, 10-Jul) still reflects a de-escalating Friday, with Monday's official not due until 13:20 London. What Friday DID say, quietly, was that the front spread had flipped to +$3 backwardation on a 287,725 t stock base, down 43.5% year-to-date: the physical market was tightening before the news broke. The counter-argument has not gone away — every overseas premium print last week was lower, EGA's 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery restarted, and China is destocking into its off-season — so this escalation is landing on an adequately supplied tape rather than a starved one. That is why the near-term skew is up but the move is headline-fragile: Washington has named its de-escalation condition (a public Iranian statement that the strait is open), and if it comes, the war premium unwinds as fast as it built. The week is dated and binary: China trade data today, US CPI Tuesday, Alcoa Thursday, the Treasury oil deadline Friday, and the first post-blockade premium assessments Friday. Until then, the single most informative number is not the flat price — it is whether Monday's official WIDENS the backwardation.

caveats: Report run Mon 13-Jul-2026, 06:05 London — BEFORE the LME ring. The latest PUBLISHED official is therefore still Fri 10-Jul (cash $3,156.00 / 3M $3,153.00 / stock 287,725 t — Westmetall). Monday's officials print at 13:20 London, after this run. The benchmark and metals-board figures are UNCHANGED from the last run because no new official has been published — not because they were carried over. Nothing in any price shown here reflects the weekend escalation.

Base-metals board basis: Westmetall 10-Jul officials for Al/Cu/Ni/Zn (day-% official-vs-official; YTD vs 02-Jan officials); the Pb row uses the 09-Jul official. Officials and closing prices are different reference series and should not be mixed.

Premiums, alumina and China data show the latest PUBLIC prints (SMM weekly 10-Jul; Platts marks 03/06-Jul) — ALL assessed BEFORE the strait was declared closed on 12-Jul. They therefore understate any escalation effect. Subscription indices (Platts / Fastmarkets) should be verified for any transaction. The Brent level (~$79) is an intraday Monday quote from press reporting, not a settlement.

Live-conflict reporting is fast-moving and contested — notably, Iran's declaration that the strait is closed is disputed by CENTCOM, and casualty/damage details vary by outlet. Sourcing reflects what Al-Monitor, CNN, Al Jazeera and CNBC carried as of run time. Elliott Wave content is technical context, not advice; probabilities are judgmental.
This report contains PUBLIC market information only. It is general market intelligence, not advice, and reflects no company's position, cost, volume or commercial data.

forward_path.basis: Latest PUBLISHED official (Westmetall); −0.25% vs 09-Jul. Struck before the strait was declared closed — Monday's official (13:20 London) is the first metal print to carry the blockade.
Latest published official; −0.50% vs 09-Jul; front spread +$3.00 BACKWARDATION. Friday's later closing print was $3,139.5.

outlook.curve_note: The front end is in BACKWARDATION (+$3.00 cash-over-3M, flipped from −$5 contango on Friday) with stock at 287,725 t and drawing daily — and that flip printed BEFORE the IRGC declared the strait closed. The curve now prices escalation-vs-repair rather than repair-vs-deficit: nearby dates are supported by physical tightness AND war risk, while the deferred curve still embeds supply normalisation (EGA's alumina restart, China exports +12–18%, Indonesia's ramp). The 2026 consensus band ($3,200–$3,500 averages) is being re-tested from below with the structural deficit (~−365 kt) as the floor. The diagnostic this week is not the flat price but the SHAPE: a widening backwardation on Monday's official confirms the physical market is repricing the blockade; a snap back to contango says the market is treating a declared closure as rhetoric.

consensus.note: Running above the World Bank base
First structural deficit in ~20 years; BofA flags near-term downside

balance.label: BofA/Morgan/BNP structural deficit; Goldman flags later surplus risk

capacity_pipeline.change: Alumina refinery RESTARTED 10-Jul (~50% within days, full capability end-2026); smelter rebuild ahead of schedule — 89 cells restarted

capacity_pipeline.timing: Restart path improves with the stand-down

capacity_pipeline.change: Output near the 45 Mt self-imposed cap; exports seen +12–18% (Fastmarkets), potentially ~7.2 Mt
capacity_pipeline.timing: Structural ceiling on output; rising exports

capacity_pipeline.change: New smelter capacity ramping (BofA flags as added supply)

scenarios.drivers: The strait stays contested — Saturday's closure declaration hardens, Gulf restarts slip, and the war premium re-prices onto a sub-290kt stock base
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the Muscat track fails to produce an Iranian 'strait open' statement; attacks on commercial shipping continue — the market re-prices the transit artery for ~9% of global / ~25% of ex-China supply
Supply: Gulf restart timelines (EGA ahead-of-schedule ramp, ALBA path) slip on renewed strikes or logistics constraints
Inventory: LME stock 287,725 t and drawing daily; the spread already flipped to backwardation BEFORE the weekend — days-of-cover critical if the export window shuts
China: output capped at 45 Mt; escalation shuts the arb window further and keeps metal home
Targets: $3,400 fast on a confirmed closure; Citi/Goldman ~$3,600; $4,000+ only on a sustained (30-day+) interdiction

scenarios.drivers: A safe-passage mechanism emerges from Muscat; sporadic incidents persist — the structural deficit floors price while supply repair and soft premiums cap it
drivers_list: Geopolitics: talks grind on (Oman/Qatar mediation) with the strait functionally open under escort/verification; a residual risk premium stays in the front of the curve
Supply: deficit persists (~−365 kt 2026) but repairs visibly — EGA alumina back, smelter cells restarting, ALBA path improving
China: exports strong (+12–18% YoY); four-year-high destocking supports the bottom; import window shut
Macro: 10Y ~4.5–4.6%, September hike odds ~64% — the rates ceiling softens only if oil behaves
Premiums: the off-season slide slows but doesn't reverse; Q4 MJP negotiates below the $395 anchor

scenarios.drivers: Tehran declares the strait open and the mechanism holds — the war premium unwinds a second time into accelerating Gulf/China/Indonesia supply and off-season demand
drivers_list: Geopolitics: the US gets its public 'strait open' statement; war-risk premia and freight surcharges unwind quickly
Supply: EGA beats its ramp plan, ALBA restarts, China exports surge, Indonesia ramps — H2 balance loosens
Premiums: the SMM weekly pattern extends — spot below settlements pulls Q4 anchors down; buyers defer
Macro: CPI (14-Jul) surprises hot → September hike odds firm → USD bid caps commodities
Technical: a daily close below $3,065 re-opens $2,990–3,020, then the .618 at $2,894

risks.note: ~9% of global and ~25% of ex-China primary supply is routed through the Gulf, ~80–85% of it via this strait. A closure that holds for weeks — not days — is the scenario in which the ~$3,855 June high is retested.
Iran's Sunday strikes hit US bases in Jordan, Kuwait, Bahrain and Oman. Bahrain and Oman host aluminium capacity; the risk has moved from the waterway to the producing countries themselves.
Washington has explicitly named this as its condition, with the Muscat track still live. The war premium built in days and can unwind in days — this move is headline-fragile in both directions.
Every overseas premium print last week was lower, EGA's 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery restarted, and China is destocking into its off-season. Escalation is landing on an adequately supplied tape.

risks.risk: Oil-led inflation tightens the rates ceiling
risks.note: A sustained ~$79+ Brent raises the odds of a hawkish CPI print and a firmer dollar — the mechanism by which a bullish supply story becomes a macro headwind for dollar-priced metals.

catalysts.event: China June trade data (incl. unwrought aluminium exports) — the week's first hard demand datapoint
LME official prints 13:20 London — the first metal mark to carry the declared Hormuz closure; watch the cash–3M spread, not the flat price
US CPI — the oil gap (~$79 Brent) makes the inflation path, and hence the rates ceiling on metals, the swing macro variable
Alcoa Q2 results (after NYSE close) — first peer read on US Midwest premium realisation under the 50% tariff and on Gulf insurance/freight costs
US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline; first post-blockade overseas premium weeklies (SMM/Platts)
An Iranian public statement declaring the strait OPEN — Washington's named de-escalation condition and the single fastest way to unwind the war premium

outlook.ai_analysis: The defining feature of 13-Jul is a timing gap, not a directional call. Oil has repriced a declared blockade; the metal's last published mark has not. Two things argue that gap closes upward: the cash–3M spread flipped into backwardation on Friday — before the news — and LME stock has drawn for thirteen straight weeks to 287,725 t, the thinnest visible cover of this cycle. A market with no buffer meets a contested transit route for a quarter of ex-China supply. One thing argues it does not close, or closes only briefly: the physical evidence of the last fortnight has been uniformly loosening — Japan spot below its own settled quarter, US Midwest drifting, Europe easing, EGA's alumina refinery back on. That is not the tape of a market about to panic. The resolution is dated. Monday's official (13:20 London) tells you whether the backwardation widens. Friday's weeklies tell you whether the premiums turn. And an Iranian statement on the strait — the condition Washington has named — could make both questions moot. Until then the skew is up and fragile: technical context, not a recommendation.

sources.label: Westmetall — LME official price and stock series
Al-Monitor — IRGC declares Strait of Hormuz closed (12-Jul)
CNBC — Oil rises as US and Iran fight for control of Hormuz (12-Jul)

premium_explainer.desc: The physical-delivery adder over the LME price: regional logistics, duties/tariffs and local tightness — quoted $/t (or ¢/lb in the US) on top of LME cash. It is what converts an exchange price into a delivered price.

Q3-2026 settled at $395/t CIF Japan (Platts, 06-Jul) — a decade high and the fourth straight rise ($86 → $195 → $351.5 → $395). SMM's 10-Jul weekly shows SPOT at $355 — below the settlement — as buyers hold back. That was assessed before Hormuz was declared closed; Q4 talks (~Sep) now open against a decade-high anchor and a re-escalating transit risk.

Platts duty-unpaid last publicly marked $525–560 (mid $542.50, 03-Jul), down ~$70 from mid-May as the war-spike unwound. The 6% EU duty, CBAM carbon costs and Mozal's care & maintenance keep the structural floor — and the war-risk freight component, which had been easing, is re-widening.

SMM's weekly DDP print eased to 109.75¢/lb ≈ $2,420/t (10-Jul); Platts MW Transaction last printed 108.7¢ (03-Jul). The 50% Section-232 tariff-$ leg (≈$1,578 at the 10-Jul LME cash) dominates the all-in — US premia track Washington and the LME, not local tightness.

The decomposition below separates the mechanical tariff leg (Section-232 rate × LME) from the residual market premium. It shows that most of the US 'premium' is a policy transfer, not a scarcity signal — and that an LME gap on Hormuz headlines lifts the US all-in automatically.

premium_outlook: Asian (MJP / CIF Japan): Q3 is settled at $395 (Platts, decade high) but spot printed $355 — below settlement — before the blockade. A formally closed strait is the one development that can arrest that slide: if a safe-passage mechanism lands, Q4 talks (~Sep) open in the LOWER half of the $340–410 band; if the closure holds, the decade-high anchor stands and spot re-rates back toward it. The 17-Jul weekly prints are the first read.

European (EDU): last public Platts mark $525–560 (03-Jul), with SMM flagging broad softness since. The 6% duty + CBAM floor plus Mozal's care & maintenance keep Europe structurally tighter than pre-war — and the marginal driver, Gulf war-risk freight, has flipped from easing back to RE-WIDENING with the Combined Maritime Forces holding the Hormuz threat level at SEVERE.

US Midwest: SMM DDP eased to 109.75¢/lb (10-Jul); the tariff leg (50% × LME ≈ $1,578) is ~two-thirds of the all-in, so US premia track the LME and Washington. A Hormuz-driven LME gap mechanically LIFTS the US all-in even with physical demand in its off-season — watch the tariff arithmetic, not the spot survey.

alumina_explainer.desc: Refined intermediate from bauxite via the Bayer process; ~1.9–2 t alumina per 1 t aluminium. Smelter-grade is the traded form.
The ex-China benchmark (Platts Alumina Price Index) referenced in many alumina contracts.
Spot index for Australian-origin smelter-grade alumina — a key seaborne reference.
The ore refined into alumina; ~2–3 t bauxite per 1 t alumina. Guinea & Australia dominate seaborne supply.
Primary Bayer reagent that dissolves alumina from bauxite — a major refining cost (~$80–120 per t of alumina).
Used for causticisation & desilication in refining — a smaller but essential reagent cost.

raw_materials.name: Bauxite (Guinea, CIF China)
raw_materials.src: ~$68 CIF China (FOB Guinea ~$38, 45/3 grade); oversupply persists — Metro Mining shipped a RECORD 1.8 Mt in Q2 (+7% YoY; 2026 guidance 6.6–7.1 Mt held; SMM 08-Jul); Guinea–EGA settlement returns CBG flows while Guinea weighs export quotas toward a >$100/dmt target (SMM/Mining.com/Discovery Alert)

raw_materials.name: Caustic soda (flakes ≥98%, FOB NE Asia)
raw_materials.src: ~$500–510 FOB NE Asia; CFR Asia ~$380–560 — major refining reagent (ChemAnalyst / ChemTradeAsia)

raw_materials.name: Lime / quicklime (CaO)
raw_materials.src: No liquid global index — largely regional/contract; indicative range (causticisation reagent)

alumina_outlook: Alumina: the surplus DEEPENS — EGA's 2.4 Mtpa Al Taweelah refinery restarted 10-Jul, removing the last supply-side tail-risk premium; expect ex-China consolidation in the $300–340 band (FOB Australia ~$340, API ~$304, Platts futures ~$309) with SHFE AO below all moving averages and port stocks building. Guinea's pending quota policy is the only clear bullish catalyst on the commodity leg.

Bauxite: Guinea oversupply keeps CIF China ~$68/dmt (FOB Guinea ~$38); record Australian shipments (Metro Mining 1.8 Mt in Q2) add length. EGA's refinery ramp is explicitly 'subject to optimisation of bauxite supply chains' — its buying returns demand to the seaborne market even as Guinea weighs a >$100/dmt export-price push.

Caustic soda / lime / freight: caustic stable (~$500/t FOB NE Asia). The delivered-cost leg is the story now: with Hormuz declared closed and the CMF threat level at SEVERE, war-risk surcharges on reagent and alumina freight into the Gulf re-widen. The commodity is cheap; getting it to a Gulf refinery is not.

inputs.name: Calcined petroleum coke (CPC, anode-grade)

inputs.name: Coal tar pitch (CTP)
inputs.src: Anode binder; by-product of steel coke ovens — supply tied to steel output; thin spot market

inputs.name: Pre-baked anodes
inputs.src: Often produced in-house; China the swing exporter — mostly contract pricing

inputs.name: Electricity (smelter power)
inputs.src: Single largest cost & the competitiveness driver; no single index — PPAs / regional grid / self-gen; rising carbon-cost exposure

inputs.name: Cryolite / bath makeup
inputs.src: Largely synthetic & recycled in-cell; limited spot market

inputs.name: Cathode / graphite blocks

inputs.name: Silicon metal
inputs.src: China Q2-26 ~$1,250 FOB; EU ~$1,580, US ~$2,625 (tariff split) — SMM/ChemAnalyst

inputs.name: Magnesium (99.9%)
inputs.src: China ~85% of global supply (~$2,359 Jan-26) — concentration risk; SMM/Asian Metal

inputs.name: Copper (LME)
inputs.src: LME-transparent leg of the input basket — Cu 3M $13,454.00 (10-Jul official, +0.3% — holding firm with the complex through the escalation headlines); busbar/cathode exposure prices off the LME

inputs.name: Ti-B grain refiner (AlTi5B1)
inputs.src: Master-alloy rod; priced off titanium + conversion — mostly contract

logistics.name: Strait of Hormuz transit
logistics.val: DECLARED CLOSED by IRGC; contested by CENTCOM
logistics.note: The IRGC Navy declared the strait closed 'until further notice' (12-Jul) after firing on a Cyprus-flagged container ship; CENTCOM disputes the closure and is striking to enforce freedom of navigation. The CMF's JMIC holds the security threat at SEVERE and has expanded the southern Omani-coast route to two-way traffic — a partial corridor, not a reopening. ~80–85% of Gulf aluminium exports and ~60% of regional alumina imports use this waterway. Treat transit as case-by-case; expect carriers and insurers to hold back (Al-Monitor / Al Jazeera / CNN).

logistics.name: Gulf war-risk insurance / surcharges
logistics.note: A confirmed missile strike on a commercial vessel plus a SEVERE threat rating is the textbook trigger for re-widened war-risk quotes, re-activated exclusion clauses and shortened quote validity. This is a delivered-cost effect that hits before any assessed premium moves, and it applies to inbound alumina/reagents as well as outbound metal.

logistics.name: Baltic Dry Index (BDI)
logistics.note: Last public print 2,944 (10-Jul, +34) — a one-month high on Capesize momentum. Note the distinction: BDI is rising on DEMAND for dry bulk (bauxite/alumina), while the war-risk component is a separate, now-widening cost line. Price the two legs separately (Baltic Exchange via Hellenic Shipping News).

logistics.name: Land-bridge (Gulf → Jeddah / Red Sea)
logistics.note: With the strait formally declared closed and only a southern corridor operating under a SEVERE threat rating, trucking-workaround readiness moves from contingency planning to live logistics: carrier capacity, costings and lead times should be current, not theoretical.

logistics.name: Incoterms / demurrage
logistics.note: A declared closure is the condition under which force-majeure and demurrage clauses on Gulf-loading contracts are actually tested. Laycan flexibility, FM language and CIF/FOB optionality are the exposures that matter this week — this is a generic contracting observation, not a position.

inputs_summary: Track closely (material AND tradeable): power (the largest cost line — Brent gapped ~4% to ~$79 Monday; EU TTF still elevated), calcined pet coke (rising, low transparency — the biggest carbon risk), silicon & magnesium (indexed, China-concentrated), copper (LME-transparent, 3M $13,454 at the last official).
Low transparency / contract (monitor via proxies): coal tar pitch, pre-baked anodes, cryolite, cathodes, Ti-B — no liquid index; track through supplier contracts and China export trends.
Highest supply-concentration risk: magnesium (~85% China), CPC (Asia / battery-anode competition), AlF₃ (fluorspar/China) — the standing candidates for forward contracting or diversification.

Logistics is the input line that changed this weekend: the commodity legs (alumina, bauxite) are cheap and getting cheaper on the EGA restart, but the DELIVERED-cost leg into and out of the Gulf is deteriorating fast — war-risk surcharges re-widening, threat level SEVERE, a single southern corridor. Cheap alumina you cannot land is not cheap alumina.

long_term.label: Long-term — weekly (position degree)
long_term.writeup: Primary (weekly): the five-wave advance from the May-2025 low (~$2,300) topped at $3,855 on 02-Jun-2026 — a Hormuz-driven fifth-wave extension, the classic commodity signature, confirmed by bearish weekly RSI divergence — and the ABC correction that followed (A $3,359 on 16-Jun, B $3,406 on 22-Jun, C $3,061.5 on 02-Jul) terminated inside the $2,950–$3,080 confluence zone where the .50 retracement and the prior fourth-wave-of-lesser-degree overlap. The three cardinal rules are intact in the impulse below (wave 2 held above the origin, wave 3 was not the shortest, wave 4 did not overlap wave 1). The recovery off $3,061.5 has been corrective in appearance so far — five official up-days, then Friday's rejection — but the declared closure of the Strait of Hormuz now supplies precisely the fundamental catalyst a wave-(1)/(iii) of a new advance requires. Base case (50%): the corrective low is IN at $3,061.5 and a multi-month recovery builds toward $3,400–$3,600 while weekly closes hold $3,050. Bull alternate (30%, raised from 25% on the blockade): a sustained closure drives price through $3,260 (.382) without the usual basing work and re-tests $3,700+. Bear alternate (20%, cut from 25%): a rapid safe-passage settlement restores the supply-repair narrative and price makes a final probe of the .618 at $2,894 before the larger recovery. Invalidation: a WEEKLY close below $2,850 voids the corrective count and re-opens $2,600. Confidence: moderate-to-good — the structure is constructive, the backwardation flip supplies physical confirmation, and the count has not required re-labelling. Technical context only, not advice.

short_term.label: Short-term — daily (swing degree)
short_term.writeup: Swing (daily): from the 02-Jul low at $3,061.5 the market built a five-wave micro-impulse into $3,210.5, then corrected as a wave-ii to the .50 retrace at $3,138 — Friday's close ($3,139.5) tagged it almost exactly, a textbook second-wave termination that respects the rule that wave 2 may not retrace 100% of wave 1. That structure now meets a fundamental catalyst (the strait declared closed, Brent ~4% higher), which is the classic setup for a third wave: the point of recognition. Base case (50%): wave iii is underway from ~$3,140 targeting $3,230 (MA20) then $3,260 (.382 of the whole decline); a CLOSING break of $3,260 projects $3,330–$3,400 as the wave-iii extension zone. Bull alternate (30%): a gap-and-go on Monday's open skips the retest entirely and runs at $3,400+ — the shape sustained blockades usually produce. Bear alternate (20%): the gap fails and the tape closes back below $3,150, which would re-label the bounce as a corrective B-wave and re-open $3,065. Invalidation: a DAILY close below $3,050 breaks the swing count outright and re-opens the $2,950–$3,000 shelf. Confidence: moderate — the count is clean and the catalyst is real, but a gap-up into a market whose physical premiums were falling last week is exactly the configuration that produces failed breakouts. Watch the cash–3M spread for confirmation: a widening backwardation validates wave iii; a snap back to contango does not.

premium_drivers.t: Hormuz formally declared CLOSED (the dominant driver)
premium_drivers.d: The IRGC Navy said the strait is closed 'until further notice' after firing on a Cyprus-flagged container ship; CENTCOM disputes the closure and is striking to enforce freedom of navigation. Roughly 9% of global and ~25% of ex-China primary supply is routed through the Gulf — this is a direct threat to the transit artery, and the reason premiums that were sliding may not keep sliding.
src: Al-Monitor — IRGC navy says Strait of Hormuz closed until further notice (12-Jul)

premium_drivers.t: Threat level SEVERE; only the southern corridor is functioning
premium_drivers.d: The Combined Maritime Forces' Joint Maritime Information Center advisory (12-Jul) holds the Hormuz security threat at SEVERE while expanding the southern Omani-coast route to two-way traffic — a partial workaround, not a reopening. Insurers and carriers price the difference: expect quote validity to shorten and war-risk surcharges to re-widen before any assessed premium moves.
src: Al Jazeera — Iran war live blog (11–12-Jul)

premium_drivers.t: US Section-232 at 50% (the policy leg dominates the US number)
premium_drivers.d: The tariff-$ leg is ≈$1,578/t at the 10-Jul LME cash ($3,156 × 50%) — roughly two-thirds of the ~$2,420 US Midwest DDP all-in. US premia therefore track the LME and Washington far more than physical tightness, and any escalation-driven LME gap raises the US delivered price automatically.
src: Federal Register — Adjusting Imports of Aluminum and Steel into the United States (50% proclamation)

premium_drivers.t: EU: 6% duty + CBAM + lost regional supply
premium_drivers.d: The 6% import duty, CBAM carbon costs and Mozal (~580 ktpa) on care & maintenance keep a structural floor under Rotterdam premia even as the June war-spike unwinds; EDU was last publicly marked $525–560 (Platts, 03-Jul) — a level set before the blockade.
src: European Commission — Carbon Border Adjustment Mechanism

premium_settlement_src: S&P Global Platts Q3-2026 MJP settlement $395/t (assessed 06-Jul-2026), via IndexBox

so_what.line: The Gulf conflict has crossed from threat to blockade: Iran's IRGC declared the Strait of Hormuz closed 'until further notice', the US answered with two waves of strikes and Iran hit US bases across four Gulf states — oil gapped ~4% to ~$79 Brent, and aluminium opens the week with the transit artery for roughly a quarter of ex-China supply formally contested.
points: What moved: Brent jumped ~3.5–4% to about $79 on Monday, snapping a two-day slide, after the weekend exchange of strikes. Aluminium's last published LME official is still Friday's (cash $3,156, 3M $3,153) — Monday's official prints at 13:20 London, after this run — so the flat price has not yet repriced the escalation.

Why it matters: Friday's officials had already flipped the cash–3M spread into a small BACKWARDATION (+$3) on a 287,725 t stock base, down 43.5% year-to-date. Fear and physical tightness are now pointing the same way for the first time since early June.

The counterweight is real: every overseas premium print last week was LOWER (Japan spot $355 against a $395 settled quarter; US Midwest ~109.75¢/lb), EGA's 2.4 Mtpa alumina refinery restarted, and China is destocking into its off-season. Escalation has to overcome an adequately supplied physical tape, not a starved one.

Watch this week: whether Iran issues the 'strait open' statement Washington has demanded (the single de-escalation trigger), Monday's LME official and whether the backwardation widens, China's June trade data (out today), US CPI Tuesday, Alcoa's Q2 on Thursday, and the US Treasury Iranian-oil wind-down deadline on Friday 17-Jul.

exec_summary: The post-conflict correction extended but began to base. LME cash settled at $3,400.00/t on Friday 19 June, down 3.8% on the week from the prior Friday close of $3,536.00 and roughly 12% below the 2 June four-year high of $3,855. The week opened soft (cash $3,419.50 Monday), printed an intraweek low of $3,359.00 on Tuesday as the ceasefire narrative dominated, then stabilised in a $3,400-3,405 band through Wednesday-Friday. Aluminum still holds about +14% year-to-date and ~+33% year-on-year. Crude eased further as the Gulf war-risk premium deflated and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) kept dollar-priced metals on the back foot - but the back-half stabilisation, not the headline drop, is the week's signal.

Under the flat-price weakness, the physical curve quietly re-tightened. The cash-to-3M spread dipped into a ~$30/t contango on Tuesday as de-escalation peaked, then flipped back to a +$2/t backwardation by Friday, while LME stocks drew a further ~4,600 t to ~315,300 t - a multi-year low near 1.3 days of global output. The US-Iran framework was formalised (MOU signed 17 June; official ceremony in Switzerland 19 June) and Hormuz is reopening, but gradually: 25 vessels transited Thursday, the most since April yet far below the ~110/day pre-war norm, and physical metal returns in months not weeks (EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline). Net read: the war premium has largely unwound, but a persistent deficit and a slow restart are cushioning the downside - a two-sided, range-building market with sharp-rebound risk if the 60-day framework slips.

price_table.note: 19 Jun settlement; week low $3,359 (16 Jun), high $3,419.5 (15 Jun); -12% from 2 Jun peak $3,855
19 Jun official; tracks cash; back-half stabilisation in a $3,398-3,410 band
Dipped to ~-$30 contango (16 Jun) at peak de-escalation, flipped back to +$2 backwardation by 19 Jun - prompt tightness reasserting
19 Jun; -4,625 t on the week; multi-yr low ~1.3 days output; still drawing - deficit persists despite peace narrative
Q2 oversupply persists; energy relief from softer oil adds pressure; Platts/Fastmarkets - verify PRA
Q2 settled $350-353/t (11-yr high, +81% QoQ from Q1 $195); Q3 talks open but Hormuz reopening now caps upside

drivers: US-Iran framework formalised: MOU signed 17 June (Versailles, post-G7), official ceremony in Switzerland Friday 19 June; mediator Pakistan confirmed the US port blockade ceases immediately and Tehran reopens Hormuz - the dominant catalyst that has now largely unwound the conflict premium.

Hormuz reopening is gradual, not instant: 25 commercial vessels transited on Thursday (the most since April) versus a ~110/day pre-war norm; transit requires registration for 'safe passage' (toll-free through the 60-day window), so physical flows normalise over weeks-to-months, not days.

Physical tightness reasserted under the headline: the cash-3M curve flipped from a mid-week ~$30 contango back to a +$2 backwardation by Friday, and LME stocks drew to ~315 kt (~1.3 days output, multi-year low) - the deficit is cushioning the flat-price downside.

Slow supply restart underpins the floor: EGA flagship ~12 months to full capacity, ALBA partially offline; Gulf pre-war supply was ~9% of global / ~25% of non-Chinese output, so the lost tonnage returns slowly even with the Strait open.

Compounding bears cap the upside: rising China output toward the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian smelter ramp, RUSAL diverting metal from China to Japan, and Guinea bauxite controls as a modest upstream offset; softer crude and a steady dollar (DXY ~100) round out the macro headwind.

implications.text: the flat-price correction has matured into a base-building range and the curve has re-tightened into backwardation, so prompt material retains a scarcity bid; use the stabilisation to layer disciplined forward hedges on priced tonnes rather than chasing fixed-price sales lower, and preserve value-added premium capture (billet/slab/PFA). Keep optionality for a sharp rebound should the 60-day framework slip.

regional premiums remain elevated (MJP Q2 $350-353/t, an 11-year high; US Midwest firm on Section 232) but a confirmed, sustained Hormuz reopening is the principal downside risk into 2H. Prioritise locking favourable Q3 MJP settlements and renewal terms now, before normalisation and RUSAL's China-to-Japan diversion feed through to premiums.

softer ex-China alumina (~$298-300/t, Q2 oversupply) caps net-seller upside; with vessels resuming Hormuz transit, begin a measured unwind of contingency-routing and war-risk insurance premiums and re-optimise CIF vs FOB exposure - but keep committed-cargo vessel cover until shipowners validate sustained safe passage (transit is rebounding but still well below pre-war volumes).

hold OTIF for strategic accounts through the transition and track the credibility/mechanics of the 60-day window, Hormuz transit counts (the real normalisation tell), EGA/ALBA restart cadence, LME stock direction and the cash-3M curve (the real tightness tell), China export/cap enforcement and the Indonesian ramp, plus US CPI/PPI and the Fed for the dollar.

outlook: Hormuz transit normalisation - whether daily vessel counts keep climbing toward the ~110/day pre-war norm and shipowners validate sustained safe passage; a stall would re-introduce a supply premium.

The cash-3M curve and LME stocks - continued draws and a holding/steepening backwardation would confirm the deficit persists despite the peace narrative; a flip back to contango would signal the unwind resuming.
Physical restart cadence - EGA/ALBA repair milestones gate how fast Gulf tonnage actually returns; the gap between visible and invisible inventories remains the key tightness risk.

Macro and competing supply - the 60-day framework's durability (slippage is the primary upside-price risk), China output against the ~45 Mt cap, the Indonesian ramp, and US CPI/PPI and the Fed for USD direction.
weekly.sources: LME official cash/3-month settlements and warehouse stock via Westmetall (week of 15-19 June: cash $3,400.0, 3M $3,398.0, stock 315,300 t on 19 Jun); benchmark CFD price (~$3,400.85 on 19 Jun, ~+33% YoY), USD/DXY, energy and macro via Trading Economics; US-Iran MOU / Strait of Hormuz reopening, 17 June signing and 19 June Switzerland ceremony, and Thursday's 25-vessel transit via Al Jazeera, NBC News, PBS, Seatrade Maritime, CNN and the UK House of Commons Library (14-19 June); EGA/ALBA restart timelines and Gulf supply share via Trading Economics and Fastmarkets; MJP Q2 premium (~$350-353/t, +81% QoQ) and RUSAL China-to-Japan diversion via S&P Global Platts, Discovery Alert and AlCircle; alumina FOB Australia via Fastmarkets and S&P Global Platts.
weekly.caveat: Note: 19 June SHFE, alumina and physical-premium figures are estimates or most-recent public anchors; daily PRA assessments (Platts/Fastmarkets/Argus) and SHFE/SMM quotes sit behind licensed feeds and should be reconciled to the desk's licensed data before pricing. This brief is market intelligence, not trading advice.
